blogPosts.post1Title: Humic Acids: A Complete Guide for Agronomists
blogPosts.post1Excerpt: What humic acids are, how they affect soil and plants, and why they are becoming the foundation of modern agriculture.
blogPosts.post1Content:
Humic acids are complex organic compounds formed during the decomposition of plant and animal residues in soil. They are the main component of humus and play a key role in soil fertility.

Main functions of humic acids:

• Improving soil structure — making heavy soils more friable and light soils more cohesive
• Increasing water retention capacity — soil retains moisture better
• Root system stimulation — plants develop a more powerful root system
• Chelation of microelements — increasing nutrient availability for plants

At GumiTechLab, we have developed a technology for extracting highly purified humic acids, ensuring maximum effectiveness of our products.
blogPosts.post2Excerpt: An overview of the latest achievements in plant biostimulants and prospects for their application in industrial agriculture.
blogPosts.post2Content:
The plant biostimulant market is growing rapidly — forecasts predict it will reach $5.3 billion by 2028. This is driven by the global trend toward sustainable agriculture and reducing chemical load on soil.

Key trends:

• Amino acid biostimulants — new formulas based on free amino acids show yield increases of up to 25%
• Combined products — combining humic substances with microorganisms for comprehensive impact
• Nanotechnology — nano-sized nutrient forms for enhanced bioavailability

Our laboratory is actively working on creating next-generation products that combine all these approaches.
blogPosts.post3Title: Soil Microbiome: Why It Matters for Your Harvest
blogPosts.post3Excerpt: How soil microorganisms affect fertility and what to do if the microbiome is disrupted.
blogPosts.post3Content:
One gram of healthy soil contains up to a billion microorganisms — bacteria, fungi, actinomycetes. This invisible world plays a critical role in plant nutrition and disease protection.

Why the microbiome matters:

• Nitrogen fixation — certain bacteria can convert atmospheric nitrogen into a form available to plants
• Phosphorus solubilization — microorganisms help plants absorb phosphorus from hard-to-reach compounds
• Biological protection — a healthy microbiome suppresses pathogenic organisms
• Humus formation — microorganisms participate in building soil fertility

At GumiTechLab, we have developed a line of microbiological products to restore and maintain a healthy soil microbiome.
